jokes.text: Break me off a piece of that... [struggling with Kit-Kat jingle]
jokes.setup: Andy confidently starts the famous Kit-Kat jingle
jokes.punchline: He can't remember the actual product name
jokes.explanation: Classic setup for Andy's try-hard personality. The confidence followed by failure is perfectly Andy.

jokes.text: Applesauce... Chrysler car... Football cream
jokes.setup: Andy continues guessing the Kit-Kat jingle
jokes.punchline: His guesses get increasingly ridiculous, ending with 'football cream'
jokes.explanation: The escalation from reasonable ('applesauce') to absurd ('football cream') is expertly paced. 'Football cream' became a fan favorite quote.

jokes.text: Even the receptionist is getting in on the creativity
jokes.setup: Pam offers to help with the commercial
jokes.punchline: Michael condescendingly reduces her to just 'the receptionist'
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Michael's oblivious condescension, though not particularly memorable.

jokes.text: MTV-on-crack kind of thing
jokes.setup: Michael describing his vision for the commercial
jokes.punchline: His idea of 'cutting edge' is outdated MTV references plus drug metaphors
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael trying to sound cool with dated references, very in character.

jokes.text: Who let the Nard dog out?
jokes.setup: Michael introduces Andy as 'the Nard Dog'
jokes.punchline: Andy immediately does his cringeworthy catchphrase pun
jokes.explanation: Predictable pun but perfectly shows Andy's desperate need for attention and catchphrases.

jokes.text: You just got Nard-dogged!
jokes.setup: Andy giving a back rub demonstration
jokes.punchline: He delivers another forced catchphrase
jokes.explanation: More forced Andy catchphrase behavior, gets old quickly but serves character development.

jokes.text: Urban? I grew up in a small town. What about me seems urban to you?
jokes.setup: Michael calls Stanley key to their 'urban vibe'
jokes.punchline: Stanley's deadpan pushback on the racial assumption
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stanley deadpan response to Michael's casual racism. Sharp, economical, and calls out the absurdity directly.

jokes.text: Stanley is hilarious
jokes.setup: Stanley just made a pointed comment about racial assumptions
jokes.punchline: Michael completely misses the criticism and thinks Stanley was joking
jokes.explanation: Michael's obliviousness to being called out is perfectly in character. The irony is thick.

jokes.text: Phyllis is like our Mrs. Butterworth. Kind of a less urban Aunt Jemima.
jokes.setup: Michael continues introducing people for the commercial
jokes.punchline: He makes an even worse racial/stereotypical comparison
jokes.explanation: More Michael casual racism/stereotyping, though less sharp than the Stanley exchange.

jokes.text: Papa Bear, Mama Bear, Baby Bear thing... Mama Bear.
jokes.setup: Michael suggests using the accountants' different sizes for a Goldilocks reference
jokes.punchline: Kevin's dejected 'Mama Bear' response to being the middle-sized one
jokes.explanation: Kevin's sad acceptance of being 'Mama Bear' is both funny and pathetic in the right way.

jokes.text: I mean, when I tell people that I work at Dunder Mifflin, they think that we sell mufflers or muffins or mittens... And frankly all of those sound better than paper, so I let it slide.
jokes.setup: Pam explaining why the commercial is good - people don't know the company
jokes.punchline: Her admission that mufflers, muffins, and mittens all sound more interesting than paper
jokes.explanation: Great alliteration with the 'M' words, and Pam's honest assessment of how boring paper is. Well-structured with perfect deadpan delivery.

jokes.text: Oh, it has losers.
jokes.setup: Dwight explains Second Life doesn't have winners or losers
jokes.punchline: Jim's simple, devastating comeback
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - four words that completely deflate Dwight's pretentious explanation. Classic Jim deadpan timing.

jokes.text: In my second life, I was also a paper salesman and I was also named Dwight. Absolutely everything was the same. Except I could fly.
jokes.setup: Dwight explaining his Second Life character
jokes.punchline: Despite having unlimited possibilities, he recreated his exact same life but with flying
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character insight - Dwight's complete lack of imagination even in a fantasy world. The 'except I could fly' is perfect deadpan punctuation.

jokes.text: Zoom back further, the hotel is actually the playground of the world's largest prison. But we zoom back further...
jokes.setup: Michael pitching increasingly complex commercial concept
jokes.punchline: Each 'zoom back' reveals something more ridiculous and impractical
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Michael's grandiose delusions, though it goes on a bit long.

jokes.text: I don't get paid by the hour anymore, but thank you. I get paid by the year.
jokes.setup: Michael says he can tell the ad exec's time is valuable
jokes.punchline: The ad exec's polite but pointed correction about salary vs hourly pay
jokes.explanation: Nice professional pushback to Michael's condescension, delivered with restraint.

jokes.text: We've been necking. But only necking. Right? Not actually kissing our mouths. Just neck on neck.
jokes.setup: Andy asking Jim for dating advice about Angela
jokes.punchline: His bizarre definition of 'necking' as literal neck-to-neck contact
jokes.explanation: Perfectly awkward Andy moment. His literal interpretation of 'necking' is both innocent and creepy.

jokes.text: Angela can stay the same, but we'll change Andy to Dwight... That's not different enough. Dwike?
jokes.setup: Andy wanting code names for their conversation
jokes.punchline: His proposed 'code name' is just a slight variation that's obviously still him
jokes.explanation: Andy's complete misunderstanding of how code names work is endearingly stupid.

jokes.text: Shrek. Shrek. I'm a donkey. I'm a donkey, Shrek.
jokes.setup: Michael prank calling Ryan pretending to be Eddie Murphy
jokes.punchline: His 'impression' is just repeating Shrek references
jokes.explanation: Very Michael to think movie quotes constitute an impression, though not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Well, actually I can't cook and I am starting a restaurant, Mike's Cereal Shack. I'm thinking we'll have as many varieties as you can buy in the store.
jokes.setup: Ryan's analogy about cooking and starting a restaurant
jokes.punchline: Michael admits he can't cook but IS starting a restaurant with just cereal
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael logic - completely missing Ryan's point while revealing an absurd business idea. 'Mike's Cereal Shack' became a fan favorite.

jokes.text: When I was five, I imagined that there was such a thing as a unicorn. And this was before I had even heard of one or seen one... Couldn't even talk yet.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to prove his creativity to Ryan
jokes.punchline: Claims he invented unicorns at age five, then says he couldn't even talk at five
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Michael delusion - claiming to have invented unicorns, then contradicting himself about being able to talk at five.

jokes.text: How about never-hundred hours, sir?
jokes.setup: Ad executives asking when they should come back
jokes.punchline: Michael's attempt at a clever 'never' response with made-up time
jokes.explanation: Michael trying to be clever with wordplay but not quite sticking the landing.

jokes.text: Ryan is being a little bitch again.
jokes.setup: Michael talking to David Wallace about the ad situation
jokes.punchline: Doesn't realize Ryan is on the call when he insults him
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael foot-in-mouth moment, very in character but predictable setup.

jokes.text: And thus Michael Scott sealed his own destiny. In a good way.
jokes.setup: Michael dramatically narrating his decision to make the ad himself
jokes.punchline: The forced addition of 'in a good way' undermines his dramatic moment
jokes.explanation: Michael's self-aware addition deflates his own dramatic moment perfectly.

jokes.text: My nickname in high school used to be Kool-Aid Man.
jokes.setup: Kevin explaining his commercial experience
jokes.punchline: His 'commercial experience' is just having a nickname based on a commercial character
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kevin logic and the visual of him as the Kool-Aid Man is amusing.

jokes.text: Sue Grafton is at the Steamtown Mall. She's doing a book signing right now.
jokes.setup: Michael asks Phyllis to find local celebrities
jokes.punchline: The only 'celebrity' available is a mystery novelist at the mall
jokes.explanation: Good setup for what passes for celebrity in Scranton, very location-specific humor.

jokes.text: Does anyone actually know what Sue Grafton looks like? I mean is she hot, or... She's crazy hot.
jokes.setup: Michael wondering if Sue Grafton is attractive
jokes.punchline: Kevin's enthusiastic confirmation that she's 'crazy hot'
jokes.explanation: Kevin's unexpected knowledge and enthusiasm about Sue Grafton's attractiveness is amusing.

jokes.text: I find the mystery genre disgusting. I hate being titillated.
jokes.setup: Discussion about mystery writer Sue Grafton
jokes.punchline: Angela's prudish rejection using 'titillated' in the most literal sense
jokes.explanation: Perfect Angela line - her prudishness and word choice create double entendre she doesn't intend.

jokes.text: What's rap?
jokes.setup: Michael assumes the song should be rap and criticizes Darryl
jokes.punchline: Darryl's deadpan 'What's rap?' in response to Michael's assumptions
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan response to Michael's racial assumptions. Two words that completely deflate Michael's stereotyping.

jokes.text: You need to learn a lot about your own culture. I'll make you a mix.
jokes.setup: Darryl just made Michael look foolish about rap assumptions
jokes.punchline: Michael doubles down on his racial assumptions
jokes.explanation: Michael's oblivious response shows he learned nothing from being schooled.

jokes.text: It's called Second Second Life for those people who want to be removed even further from reality.
jokes.setup: Jim explaining Dwight's Second Life character has his own world
jokes.punchline: The recursive absurdity of Second Second Life
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation of the Second Life concept. The recursive nature and Jim's matter-of-fact delivery make it perfect.

jokes.text: You're a sports writer in Philadelphia? Nice build, too... I did not know you played guitar.
jokes.setup: Pam discovering Jim's detailed Second Life avatar
jokes.punchline: Her growing realization of how much time Jim spent creating his avatar
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Pam catching Jim in his own version of Dwight's escapism.

jokes.text: I don't hate it. I just don't like it at all, and it's terrible.
jokes.setup: Michael being confronted about hating the jingle
jokes.punchline: His contradictory attempt to soften his criticism
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael logic - trying to seem diplomatic while being completely insulting.

jokes.text: Break me off a piece of that lumber tar... Snickers bar... Grey Poupon
jokes.setup: Andy still trying to remember the Kit-Kat jingle
jokes.punchline: His continued wrong guesses, now including 'lumber tar'
jokes.explanation: Good callback with 'lumber tar' being particularly absurd. Running gag payoff.

jokes.text: So I kept on asking, and they finally threw me out of the store in front of all my friends.
jokes.setup: Phyllis explaining she wasn't supposed to take no for an answer from Sue Grafton
jokes.punchline: Her literal interpretation led to public humiliation
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Phyllis taking Michael's instructions too literally with embarrassing results.

jokes.text: Piss or get off the pot!
jokes.setup: People are leaving and Jim is deciding whether to stay
jokes.punchline: Creed's blunt, inappropriate encouragement
jokes.explanation: Very Creed - inappropriate but somehow fitting advice delivered with his typical bluntness.

jokes.text: I passed out on my keyboard trying to decide.
jokes.setup: Pam explaining her difficult choice between driving tired or staying with Michael and Dwight
jokes.punchline: She literally fell asleep while weighing the options
jokes.explanation: Good Pam deadpan delivery and relatable exhaustion humor.

jokes.text: Oh, D. Oh, D.
jokes.setup: Andy describing Angela's passionate response during their encounter
jokes.punchline: She was saying 'Oh, D' which Andy thinks means Andy (D for Andy)
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup for the reveal that Angela was thinking of Dwight. Andy's obliviousness makes it tragic and funny.

jokes.text: D for Andy.
jokes.setup: Jim asking for clarification about the 'D' nickname
jokes.punchline: Andy's illogical explanation that D stands for Andy
jokes.explanation: Andy's complete lack of logic in making D equal Andy is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: [Jim's dawning realization reaction]
jokes.setup: Andy explains that Angela called him 'D'
jokes.punchline: Jim's silent realization that Angela was thinking of Dwight
jokes.explanation: Perfect visual comedy moment. Jim's reaction carries all the comedy - his horror at Andy's obliviousness is palpable.

jokes.text: People are stupid. People like waving. Waving sells, not art.
jokes.setup: Michael's commercial was rejected in favor of the corporate version
jokes.punchline: His bitter dismissal of audiences and simple commercial tactics
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Michael's artistic pretensions when his work is rejected.

jokes.text: In a fast-moving world, where good news moves at the speed of time, and bad news isn't always what it seems.
jokes.setup: Michael's pretentious commercial voiceover
jokes.punchline: The meaningless phrase 'speed of time' and other pseudo-profound nonsense
jokes.explanation: Perfect parody of pretentious commercial copy. 'Speed of time' is wonderfully meaningless.

jokes.text: Break me off a piece of that Fancy Feast. It's the cat food. Nailed it.
jokes.setup: Andy has been struggling with the Kit-Kat jingle all episode
jokes.punchline: He finally 'gets it' but with cat food instead of Kit-Kat
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback payoff. Andy's confidence in getting it wrong is perfect. 'Fancy Feast' became hugely quotable.
